one_line_takeaway: A rigorous theory showing that inference-time reward hacking (rise-then-collapse of true reward) is inevitable for Best-of-n-style selection, plus two practical fixes: Best-of-Poisson, a single-parameter near-optimal approximation to the RLHF-optimal tilt, and HedgeTune, a root-finding rule that recovers the true-reward-maximizing operating point.
key_finding: Hedging via HedgeTune consistently recovers the true-reward-maximizing operating point and mitigates hacking. In verifiable setups the observed accuracy-vs-n curves match Theorem 1's predicted single-peak shape and then decline — e.g. BoN hacks on GPQA even with the strong Skywork-Llama-3.1 8B proxy (ranked 12th on RewardBench) — and HedgeTune recovers the best n for BoN/BoP and a superior reward-distortion curve for SBoN in all tested cases. BoP is shown to approximate the intractable optimal reward-tilted (RLHF-optimal) distribution with a KL gap of order 1e-4 (numerically bounded between 0 and 8e-4) using a single parameter, making it a cheap inference-time stand-in for RLHF that can traverse the whole reward-KL frontier without retraining.
core_contribution: The paper studies reward hacking (over-optimization of a misspecified proxy reward at the expense of the true objective) specifically in the INFERENCE-TIME alignment setting, where multiple candidate outputs are sampled and selected by reward score. Its three own contributions are: (1) a mathematical formalization of inference-time reward hacking (Definition 1) plus an 'inevitability' result (Theorem 1 / Corollary 1) showing that for any strictly-TP2 / monotone-likelihood-ratio family of selection distributions with an increasing score function, the true-reward-vs-parameter curve f(theta) is either monotone or has exactly one interior extremum — so the empirically observed 'rise then collapse' hacking pattern is unavoidable for BoN, BoP, and exponential-family tilts; (2) Best-of-Poisson (BoP, Algorithm 3), a NEW inference-time method that runs Best-of-n with the sample count n = n'+1 drawn from a Poisson(mu), yielding a single-parameter family whose induced density q_mu(x)=(mu*x+1)e^{mu(x-1)} on [0,1] approximates the optimal KL-constrained reward-tilted distribution to within a tiny KL gap; (3) HedgeTune (Algorithm 4), a one-dimensional root-finding procedure that locates the optimal inference-time parameter (n for BoN, inverse-temperature lambda for SBoN, mu for BoP) by solving the residual condition E[r_t(u)*psi(u,theta)]=0, i.e. the point where the marginal benefit of further proxy optimization hits zero.
threat_model: Not an adversarial-attacker threat model. The 'threat' is proxy-reward misspecification: reward models are imperfect proxies for latent true objectives (correctness, helpfulness, safety), so aggressively selecting the highest-proxy-scoring sample (as in Best-of-n) invokes the winner's curse — selection systematically favors outputs where the proxy OVERESTIMATES true quality, degrading true performance past a 'hacking threshold'. This is an AI-safety/alignment robustness concern (Goodhart's law, specification gaming), which the authors explicitly frame as socio-technical safety, not a defense against an external adversary. Assumed capability for the mitigation: one-time black-box calibration access to the true reward (e.g. verifiable answers, a stronger reward model, or LLM-as-judge) to tune theta.
limitations: (1) The core mitigation REQUIRES black-box access to the true reward for a one-time calibration — practical only where verifiable rewards, a stronger RM, or an LLM-judge exist; if no such signal is available the method cannot tune theta (authors note that when the proxy is always at odds with the truth, the optimal policy is just the reference, lambda=0). (2) The clean theory rests on Assumption 1 (uniform reward mapping) and TP2/MLR regularity; the continuous-uniform idealization introduces (claimed negligible) error and the general discrete case is deferred to the appendix. (3) BoP's exact optimal-tilt approximation guarantee is derived for uniformly distributed rewards. (4) The human-preference experiments use small models (Pythia 1.4B reference, 44M proxy RMs) and a simulated-gold-reward setup, so real-world human-preference generality is not directly established. (5) The framework tunes a single scalar parameter per prompt-set; it does not address multi-dimensional or context-dependent reward conflicts beyond noting they exist.

datasets_used: Preference Proxy Evaluations (PPE) dataset [Frick et al. 2025] — multiple responses per question from frontier models (GPT-4o-mini, Claude Haiku 3), scored by a suite of reward models; used only on benchmark/RM pairs PPE flags as hacking
tlc4418/gold_labelled_gens [Coste et al. 2024] — 12,600 responses for each of 1,000 AlpacaFarm-validation prompts, scored by AlpacaRM
AlpacaFarm dataset (source of prompts; reference model fine-tuned on it)
quantitative_results: BoP approximates the optimal reward-tilted distribution with a KL divergence gap of order 1e-4, numerically bounded between 0 and 8e-4 across all mu (for uniformly distributed rewards)
GPQA + Skywork-Llama-3.1 8B (verifiable, Fig 3): accuracy varies over ~43.0%-45.5% across n=5..30, exhibiting a hacking peak that HedgeTune recovers
Human-preference setup (Fig 4): expected true reward rises to roughly ~0.10-0.20 then declines with n for BoN/BoP; SBoN reaches the peak without collapsing
Proxy reward models trained on preference-pair datasets of size 10k, 20k, 46k, 80k; annotator-disagreement simulated at 0% and 25% label noise; all runs repeated over 4 random seeds
Skywork-Llama-3.1 8B is noted as the 12th-best non-generative reward model on RewardBench
Closed-form BoP results (Thm 2): E[X_BoP] = 1 - 1/(mu+1) - e^{-mu}/mu^2; KL(pi_BoP || pi_ref) = e^{-mu-1}(Ei(mu+1)-Ei(1))/mu + log(mu+1) - 1
baselines_compared: Best-of-n (BoN) sampling — standard, un-tuned baseline that hacks at large n
Soft Best-of-n (SBoN) [Verdun et al. 2025] — temperature-softmax generalization of BoN (prior work by overlapping authors), used as a hedging baseline
Best-of-Poisson (BoP) — the paper's own proposed method
The optimal KL-constrained reward-tilted distribution (exponential tilt / RLHF-optimal policy) — intractable theoretical ideal used as the reference frontier
Each method with vs without HedgeTune tuning (BoN, BoN-HedgeTune, SBoN-HedgeTune, BoP-HedgeTune)
